coredata.dc:title: Tuning the aggregation of silver nanoparticles with carbon dots for the surface-enhanced Raman scattering application
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Mingming
Du, Bowen
Wang, Qian
Zhang, Jiaxin
Zhu, Xiajun
Lin, Zhengyu
Dong, Yongqiang
Fu, FengFu
Yu, Ting
coredata.dc:description: Single-layer carbon dots (CDs) are used as capping agents to synthesize silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). The obtained nanohybrids (AgNPs/CDs) can be self-assembled into nanostructures like nanochains (1D-AgNPs/CDs), nanoflats (2D-AgNPs/CDs) or nanobodys (3D-AgNPs/CDs) by simply tuning the amount of added CDs. It is found that CDs play a key role in controlling the aggregation AgNPs/CDs. The formation mechanisms of the AgNPs/CDs aggregates have been discussed. On the basis, the effects of aggregation dimension on the surface plasmon absorption and surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) activity of AgNPs/CDs are investigated and discussed. It is found that the aggregation of AgNPs/CDs creates strong localized surface plasmon resonance. Furthermore, the aggregated AgNPs/CDs of different dimension have similar absorption intensity in the range from 500 to 800 nm, which is most commonly used in the surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) measurement. The outstanding local electromagnetic field of the aggregated AgNPs and the enrichment effect of the CDs towards the analytes make all the obtained materials to be SERS substrates. In particular, 2D-AgNPs/CDs exhibit an ultrahigh enhancement factor of 4.02 × 1014 and a good reproducibility during the SERS test, using crystal violet as the model target molecule. The applicability of 2D-AgNPs/CDs in SERS detection is further confirmed by the measurement of trace thiram residual in apple.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silver nanoparticles
Carbon dots
Self-assembly
Dimension
Surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection the trace thiram in apple
Synthesis, characterization and formation mechanism of AgNPs/CDs with different aggregation dimensions
LSPR properties of AgNPs/CDs with different aggregation dimensions
SERS performances of AgNPs/CDs with different aggregation dimentions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Typical TEM (a, b, c), SEM (d, e, f) and HRTEM (g, h, i) images of the 1D-AgNPs/CDs (a, d, g), 2D-AgNPs/CDs (b, e, h) and 3D-AgNPs/CDs (c, f, i). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Diagram for the preparation of 1D-AgNPs/CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

UV–vis absorption spectra of the all the four AgNPs/CDs samples. The concentrations of all the four samples are 40 μg/mL (a); The absorption cross section of the AgNPs cluster calculated by FDTD (b). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

SERS spectra of CV of different concentrations on 2D-AgNPs/CDs (a); The concentration dependence of SERS intensity for CV at 1623 cm−1 (b); Raman spectra of bulk CV, and SERS spectra of CV solution dropped on the four kinds of AgNPs/CDs (c). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

SERS spectra of thiram in (a) water and (b) apple juice with its concentration ranging from 0.01 to 1000 ppb collected from the 2D-AgNPs/CDs. Corresponding logarithmic SERS intensity of thiram 1384 cm−1 as a function of the logarithmic thiram concentration (c, d). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Results of recovery experiments for detecting thiram using the 2D-AgNPs/CDs coupled with the SERS technique.

head.ce:title: Tuning the aggregation of silver nanoparticles with carbon dots for the surface-enhanced Raman scattering application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: MOE Key Laboratory for Analytical Science of Food Safety and Biology, Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection Technology for Food Safety, College of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MOE Key Laboratory for Analytical Science of Food Safety and Biology
Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection Technology for Food Safety

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: MOE Key Laboratory for Analytical Science of Food Safety and Biology, Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Analysis and Detection Technology for Food Safety, College of Chemistry, Fuzhou University, Fuzhou, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Physics and Applied Physics School of Physical and Mathematical Sciences, Nanyang Technological University, 637371, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Division of Physics and Applied Physics School of Physical and Mathematical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Division of Physics and Applied Physics School of Physical and Mathematical Sciences, Nanyang Technological University, 637371, Singapore

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Single-layer carbon dots (CDs) are used as capping agents to synthesize silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). The obtained nanohybrids (AgNPs/CDs) can be self-assembled into nanostructures like nanochains (1D-AgNPs/CDs), nanoflats (2D-AgNPs/CDs) or nanobodys (3D-AgNPs/CDs) by simply tuning the amount of added CDs. It is found that CDs play a key role in controlling the aggregation AgNPs/CDs. The formation mechanisms of the AgNPs/CDs aggregates have been discussed. On the basis, the effects of aggregation dimension on the surface plasmon absorption and surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) activity of AgNPs/CDs are investigated and discussed. It is found that the aggregation of AgNPs/CDs creates strong localized surface plasmon resonance. Furthermore, the aggregated AgNPs/CDs of different dimension have similar absorption intensity in the range from 500 to 800 nm, which is most commonly used in the surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) measurement. The outstanding local electromagnetic field of the aggregated AgNPs and the enrichment effect of the CDs towards the analytes make all the obtained materials to be SERS substrates. In particular, 2D-AgNPs/CDs exhibit an ultrahigh enhancement factor of 4.02 × 1014 and a good reproducibility during the SERS test, using crystal violet as the model target molecule. The applicability of 2D-AgNPs/CDs in SERS detection is further confirmed by the measurement of trace thiram residual in apple.

The obtained CDs shelled AgNPs (AgNPs/CDs) can be self-assembled into 1D-, 2D- or 3D-CDs/AgNPs depending on the amount of CDs, and all the CDs/AgNPs aggregations have strong surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) activities.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Surface enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) is a powerful technique that can greatly enhance the Raman signals of molecules absorbed on some specially designed substrates [1] SERS has become one of the most interesting and active research areas, due to its broad applications in both fundamental research and sensing [2,3]. As a surface sensitive technique, the sensitivity of SERS is substantially dependent on the substrate. Therefore, various SERS substrates have been prepared to chase higher SERS activities, according to electromagnetic enhancement and/or chemical enhancement mechanisms [4–6].

Ag based nanostructures are considered to be the most desirable candidates for SERS substrate due to the strong localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) [7]. In particular, the metal nanoparticles with array arrangement were strongly enhanced the LSPR intensity, especially the configuration of two or more Ag nanoparticles (AgNPs) separated by a gap, which were theoretically and experimentally confirmed as “hot spot” [8,9]. And an incident electromagnetic field can be generated and effectively amplify the near field between and around the nanostructures, greatly enhancing the Raman signal [10]. In general, these AgNPs structures could be prepared through direct fabrication or post-assembly. The assembly methods are usually more convenient than those fabrication methods, and have attracted broad attention [11,12]. Many assembly strategies have been developed, such as electrostatic interaction method [13,14], DNA hybridization-driven method [15], and molecular interactions method [16,17], to prepare aggregated AgNPs. These strategies provide efficient ways to tune down the distance between two AgNPs to 1 nm or less. Unfortunately, these methods are usually complex, and can't well control the assembly dimension of the AgNPs. As a result, a systematic study about the effect of aggregation dimension on the SERS activity of AgNPs is still absent, and the SERS performance of Ag based substrate is difficult to be further improved.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a group of novel carbon based nanomaterials which are composed of sp2 carbon cores and surface functional groups [18]. CDs exhibit many unique properties, such as fluorescence, electrochemiluminescence and catalysis [18]. Therefore, CDs have been well applied in sensing and imaging [19,20]. In addition, the ultra-thin layer structure, tiny lateral sizes and abundant surface functional groups make CDs to be ideal capping agents to synthesize metal nanoparticles [3,21,22]. In this work, single-layer carbon dots (CDs) are used as the capping agents to develop a simple method to prepare CDs capped AgNPs (AgNPs/CDs). More importantly, the obtained AgNPs/CDs can be self-assembled into nanochains (1D-AgNPs/CDs), nanoflats (2D-AgNPs/CDs) or nanobodys (3D-AgNPs/CDs) by tuning the amount of added CDs. It is found that the aggregation dimension affects greatly the LSPR of AgNPs, and also the SERS activity. 2D-AgNPs/CDs show the best SERS activity with an ultrahigh enhancement factor of 4.02 × 1014, and are applied to detect the trace thriam residual in apple.

Single-layer CDs were synthesized according to a reported method [22]. In brief, 10 g of wood based activated carbon was refluxed with 300 mL of 8 M HNO3 at 130 °C for 48 h. After that, the suspension was cooled to room temperature and filtered through a 220 nm filter. The filtrate was collected and dried by reduced pressure distillation. Finally, about 6 g CDs powder was obtained. The lateral sizes of the CDs are mainly distributed in the range of 3–8 nm, with an average value of about 5 nm (see Fig. S1). The heights of the CDs are mainly distributed in the range of 0.4–1.0 nm, with an average value of about 0.6 nm (see Fig. S2).

1D-AgNPs/CDs were synthesized by a one-pot self-assembly method. In a typically experiment, 1500 μL of CD solution (5 mg/mL) and 600 μL of AgNO3 solution (1 mol/L) were added to 50 mL of DI water. The solution was stirred for 5 min, allowing the composition among CDs and Ag+ ions. Then, the pH value of the solution was adjusted to about 9, using NaOH solution (0.1 mol/L). Subsequently, the solution was refluxed at 120 °C for 15 min. After that, 400 μL of glucose solution (90 mg/mL) was added rapidly into the boiling solution under stirring. The boiling was kept for 20 min, allowing the Ag+ ions were well reduced by the glucose. After that, the heating was stopped, allowing the solution was cooled naturally to room temperature while the stirring was continued. Subsequently, the cooled mixture was centrifuged at 12,000 rmp for 10 min 3 times. Finally, the precipitate (1D-AgNPs/CDs) was re-dispersed in 10 mL DI water and stored at 4 °C for further use. The synthesis processes of 2D-AgNPs/CDs and 3D-AgNPs/CDs were same with that of 1D-AgNPs/CDs, only changing the dosages of the reactants. The volume of CD solution used for the synthesis of 2D-AgNPs/CDs and 3D-AgNPs/CDs was 1200 μL and 600 μL, respectively.

2 mL of cleared 2D-AgNPs/CDs solution was re-dispersed in 50 mL of DI water, which was heated to boiling for 10 mL accompanied with violent stirring. Then the solution was cooled to room temperature, allowing the stirring was continued. Finally, 0D-AgNPs/CDs was obtained.

The finite-different time-domain (FDTD) was used to simulate the scattering cross section of the Ag nanoparticles in two dimensions using the experimental dielectric function from Palik (0–2 μm) for the Ag material. The simulation structure consists of the 20 nm diameter sized Ag nanoparticles with 1 nm gap among each other. The simulation the domain includes structure with perfect matched layers in all directions. The refractive index of SiO2 is n = 1.45. For the calculation, the simulated electromagnetic fields include Ag nanoparticles and the silicon wafer substrate.

Silicon (Si) wafers of 0.5 × 0.5 cm were cleaned for 5 min in acetone, ethanol, and DI water sequentially by ultra-sonication. Then, the cleaned Si wafer was treated in a solution of 98% H2SO4/30% H2O2 = 4:1 (v/v) for 4 h to obtain the hydroxyl surface. The hydroxylized Si wafers were subsequently immersed in 0.05 M CTAB solution for 24 h, allowing the self-assemble of CTAB bilayer on the surface of Si wafers. Then, the CTAB modified Si wafers were washed with DI to remove excess molecules. After that, 10 μL of AgNPs/CDs (10 μg/mL) was dropped on the modified Si wafes, and dried at 60 °C for 3 min to obtain SERS substrates. Finally, 20 μL of crystal violet or thiram solution of different concentrations was dropped on the SERS substrate, and dried at 60 °C for 3 min. Thus, the SERS detection could be carried out.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection the trace thiram in apple
ce:section.ce:para: A local (FuZhou, China) purchased apple was used as the real sample to test the feasibility and reliability of the developed SERS sensor. 50 g of edible parts of the apple were homogenized and mixed with 50 mL DI water. The mixture was juicer for 5 min, and then centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min. The obtained supernatant was further filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove insoluble residues present. Thereafter, Aliquots (50 μL) of the apple juice sample were spiked with standard thiram solutions (50 μL; final concentration, 0.01 ppb - 1 ppm), and then detect the SERS signals of thiram using the method mentioned above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis, characterization and formation mechanism of AgNPs/CDs with different aggregation dimensions
ce:section.ce:para: Single-layer CDs obtained from a “top-down” method are used as the capping agents to synthesize AgNPs/CDs. As shown in Fig. 1 , TEM and SEM images indicate that a lot of spherical nanoparticles with uniform sizes of about 20 ± 3 nm have been formed. HRTEM images indicate that the AgNPs have clear lattice fringes with distance of about 0.200 nm and 0.223 nm (see the inset in Fig. 1g), corresponding to the (200) and (111) facets of AgNPs, respectively [23,24]. The lattice fringes of the AgNPs suggest a good crystallinity, which can be proved by the XRD patterns (Fig. S3). All the samples show four typical peaks at 38, 44, 64 and 78°, which are respectively assigned to the (111), (200), (220) and (311) reflections of face-centered cubic (fcc) AgNPs. Thus, the spherical nanoparticles should be AgNPs. The TEM and HRTEM images also show a lot of small dots around the AgNPs. The sizes of the samll dots fit well with the CDs. Thus, it can be infered that the obtained materials should be AgNPs/CDs. The conclusion is also supported by the experimental results from XPS and FTIR spectra. All the XPS spectra of the samples show a peak for carbon (C1s at 284.0 eV), a peak for oxygen (O1s at 531.8 eV) and four peaks for silver (Ag3d5/2 at 367.6 eV, Ag3d3/2 at 373.6 eV, Ag3p3/2 at 573.1 eV and Ag3p1/2 at 603.6 eV). XPS spectra suggest that the materials are mainly composed of carbon, oxygen and sliver elements (Fig. S4) [25,26]. The high resolution XPS spectra of the C1s peak of all the four samples show the peaks for C-C (sp2), C-C (sp3), C-O, O=C-OH bonds (Fig. S5), suggesting the presence of sp2 C-C structures and lots of oxygen-containing functional groups (mainly carboxyl group) [21,23]. The conclusion can be further confirmed by the FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. S6). All the samples show the absorption bands of O-H, C=O, C=C and C-O bonds [21]. The results mentioned above indicate that the obtained AgNPs should be capped with CDs.

Interestingly, the obtained materials are AgNPs/CDs aggregates rather than monodispersive AgNPs/CDs (Fig. 1). The aggregation dimension of the obtained materials is mainly dependent on the amount of the added CDs. When the amount of CDs is decreased gradually, the dimension of the obtained AgNPs/CDs aggregates increases from 1 to 3. TEM and SEM images show that the 1D-AgNPs/CDs are mainly some nanochains (Fig. 1 a, d), the 2D-AgNPs/CDs are mainly some single-layer nanoflats (Fig. 1b, e), and the 3D-AgNPs/CDs are mainly some nanobodies (Fig. 1c, f). It can be clearly found that all the 1D-, 2D- and 3D-AgNPs/CDs are composed of dozens/hundreds of AgNPs/CDs. The HRTEM images also reveal that lots of small gaps are present between neighboring particles (Fig. 1g, h, i). Most of the gaps are below 2 nm, mainly distributed in the range of 0.8–1.5 nm.

It is worthy discussing the formation of the aggregated AgNPs/CDs. Thus, two control experiments were carried out. In one contral experiment, AgNPs were synthesized in the absence of CDs, according to the same process of 1D-AgNPs/CDs. The results indicate that the AgNPs aggregated seriously, and aggregates show poor dispersity in water due to less of capping agent. In the other control experiment, trisodium citrate was used instead of CDs as the capping agent. The experimental results show that the obtained AgNPs are monodispersive, no matter how to regulate the ratio of trisodium citrate and AgNO3. Apparently, CDs should play a key role in the aggregation of AgNPs/CDs. As mentioned above, the functional groups of CDs are mainly carboxyl groups, which are same with those of trisodium citrate. Therefore, the aggregation of AgNPs/CDs may be related to the special structure of CDs rather than the functional groups. The used CDs have lateral sizes of 4 ± 2 nm, and a large number of carboxyl groups are distributed mainly on the edges of CDs. Therefore, a CD sheet may connect with two AgNPs at the same time through different carboxyl groups (as shown in Fig. 1a). Then AgNPs/CDs can be aggregated. On the other hand, all the AgNPs/CDs are negatively charged due to the carboxyl groups of the capped CDs. The electrostatic repulsion among the conn ected AgNPs/CDs make the particles leave each other as far as possible (as shown in Fig. 2 ). As a result, the connected AgNPs/CDs are arranged in line, forming some nanochains (1D-AgNPs/CDs). When the amount of AgNO3 is increased gradually, more and more AgNPs are produced, and more and more AgNPs may be connected together by CDs. Then 2D- and 3D-AgNPs/CDs can be formed. It should be mentioned that the connection among AgNPs and CDs should be resulted from the complexation effect between Ag+ ions on the surface of AgNPs and carboxyl group on CDs. The connection is stable at room temperature. Therefore, the aggregated AgNPs/CDs have a long-term stability in solution. However, when aggregated AgNPs/CDs solution is diluted and heated to boiling, the connection may be broken due to the hydrolysis of Ag+. And the aggregated AgNPs/CDs disassembled into monodisperive AgNPs/CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LSPR properties of AgNPs/CDs with different aggregation dimensions
ce:section.ce:para: It has been found that the aggregation of AuNPs or AgNPs would generate a strong LSPR due to the electromagnetic coupling among closely spaced particles [7,27]. However, the present studies are mainly focused on 1D or disorderly aggregates because of the difficulty to control efficiently the dimension of the aggregates. Here, AgNPs/CDs of different aggregation dimensions can be easily tuned by controlling the mount of CDs. Furthermore, the AgNPs/CDs aggregates can be easily broken into monodisperive AgNPs/CDs (0D-AgNPs/CDs) by adding the AgNPs/CDs aggregates into boiling water (Fig. S7). Thus, AgNPs/CDs of 0D, 1D, 2D and 3D can be all obtained. Therefore, this study provides an ideal mode for studying the effect of aggregating dimension of AgNPs on the LSPR. The four samples show obviously different solution colors (see the inset of Fig. 3 a), suggesting different LSPR [28,29]. Therefore, the UV–vis spectra of the.

samples are mesaured. The as-obtained 0D-AgNPs/CDs exhibit a sharp peak centered at around 395 nm, and a small peak at around 605 nm. The sharp peak at 395 nm is quite similar with those of monodisperse spherical AgNPs [21,23], and should be assigned to the 0D-AgNPs/CDs. The small absorption peak at 605 nm may imply a small quantity of aggregates, which can be observed in the SEM image (Figure S7 c). As the AgNPs/CDs aggregate into 1D-AgNPs/CDs, the former peak turns broader, and the latter peak is enhanced greatly and red-shifted to 635 nm. Similar phenomenon has been observed by other groups [30–32]. Thus, the two peaks should be characteristic absorption bands of 1D-AgNPs/CDs. As the case of 2D-AgNPs/CDs, the main absorption peaks at around 395 and 630 nm are quite similar with those of 1D-AgNPs/CDs. However, the absorption range is further extended to 1350 nm. At the same time, three new and weak absorption bands are presented in the range of 750–965 nm, 965–1150 nm and 1150–1350 nm, respectively. When the materials are further aggregated into 3D-AgNPs/CDs, the absorption spectrum is quite similar with that of 2D-AgNPs/CDs. However, the relative intensities of the three bands in the range from 750 to 1350 nm are further increased.

To further investigate the mechanism of aggregation dimension on the LSPR of AgNPs, we use the finite-difference time-domain (FDTD) method to calculate absorption cross section of the AgNPs cluster with 1 nm gap among each other (Fig. 3b). An obvious LSPR peak can be found near 420 nm, which is similar with the absorption peak of 2D-AgNPs/CDs in Fig. 3a. Besides, an additional band appears at the shorter wavelength side of the peak, such discrepancy is due to nonconstant gap distance between two AgNPs in the experiment. The field intensity (|E|2) distribution was also calculated on the cross sections parallel to the substrate for excitation at λ = 420 nm (Fig. S8), in this way, we can see that the strongest field located at the gap between two AgNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SERS performances of AgNPs/CDs with different aggregation dimentions
ce:section.ce:para: Apparently, the aggregation of AgNPs/CDs enhances greatly the surface plasmon absorption. In particular, the absorbance of the three aggregated cases is much stronger than that of 0D-AgNPs/CDs in the range of 500–800 nm, which is most commonly used in the SERS measurement. Therefore, the three kinds of aggregated AgNPs/CDs should exhibit much better SERS activities than that of 0D-AgNPs/CDs. However, it is difficult to judge directly which will show the best SERS performance due to no significant different absorbance in the wavelength range. Therefore, the SERS performances of the four materials are investigated by using crystal violet (CV) as the probe molecule. Strong Raman signal of CV can be detected on all the four materials. For 0D-AgNPs/CDs, there is a good semi-logarithmic correlation between the SERS intensity at 1623 cm− 1 and the concentration of CV in the range from 1 × 10−9 to 1 × 10−5 M (Fig. S9), with a detection limit of 1 × 10−9 M. The results are not so good for an Ag based SERS substrate [15]. However, the three cases of aggregated AgNPs/CDs give us surprising results. For both 1D-AgNPs/CDs and 3D-AgNPs/CDs, the linear response range of CV is 1 × 10−16–1 × 10−12 M, and the detection limit is 1 × 10−16 M (Figs. S10 and S11). For the 2D-AgNPs/CDs, the linear response range is 1 × 10−18–1 × 10−14 M (Fig. 4 a and b), and the detection limit is as low as 1 × 10−18 M. To further evaluate the SERS performances of the four substrates, the EFs are measured (Fig. 4c). The calculated EFs are shown in Table S1. It can be seen that the EFs of the aggregated AgNPs/CDs are much higher than that of 0D-AgNPs/CDs, and the EF of 2D-AgNPs/CDs is higher slightly than those of 1D-AgNPs/CDs and 3D-AgNPs/CDs. Apparently, 2D-AgNPs/CDs show the best SERS activity when excited with a 532 nm laser, which may be related to the relative strong surface plasmon absorption at that wavelength.

The point-to-point relative standard deviation (RSD) in one batch of 1D-AgNPs/CDs, 2D-AgNPs/CDs or 3D-AgNPs/CDs is determined by detecting the SERS intensity of CV (1 × 10−14 mol/L) from 50 sites on one substrate. The RSD value is calculated to be 6.37%, 1.70% and 4.09%, respectively (Fig. S12). The SERS spectra of CV (1 × 10−14 mol/L) at 50 random sites from five batches of the 1D-AgNPs/CDs, 2D-AgNPs/CDs and 3D-AgNPs/CDs are also determined. The RSD is 8.26%、3.85% and 6.03%, respectively (Fig. S13). The results indicate that 2D-AgNPs/CDs also show the best repeatability and reproducibility. That may be resulted from the good uniformity of the materials on the surface of silicon wafer (Figs. S14 and S15). The experimental results mentioned above suggest that 2D-AgNPs/CDs show the best SERS performance.

An environmentally friendly and one-step method has been developed to prepare AgNPs/CDs, which can be self-assembled into 1D-, 2D- or 3D-AgNPs/CDs by tuning the amount of CDs. The three kinds of aggregated AgNPs/CDs show strong LSPR in the range of 500–800 nm, which are most commonly used in the SERS detection. The strong LSPR and the abundant nanogaps of the aggregated AgNPs/CDs make them have excellent SERS activities. Among the three aggregated materials, 2D-AgNPs/CDs are found to be ideal SERS substrate materials due to the highest EF and the best reproducibility. Finally, the applicability of 2D-AgNPs/CDs as a SERS substrate is confirmed by the measurement of trace thiram in apple.

Mingming Chen: designed and carried out the detailed experiments, analysed the data and wrote the whole paper. Bowen Du: designed and carried out the detailed experiments, analysed the data and wrote the whole paper. Qian Wang: designed and carried out the detailed experiments, analysed the data and wrote the whole paper. Jiaxin Zhang: designed and carried out the detailed experiments, analysed the data and wrote the whole paper. Xiajun Zhu: designed and carried out the detailed experiments, analysed the data and wrote the whole paper. Zhengyu Lin: planed and supervised the project. Yongqiang Dong: planed and supervised the project. FengFu Fu: planed and supervised the project. Ting Yu: planed and supervised the project.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21675026, 21976029), the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2020Y0074), National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFC1600500), and the Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (No. IRT1116).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2021.09.039.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Probing single molecules and single nanoparticles by surface-enhanced Raman scattering

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Nie, S. R. Emory, Probing single molecules and single nanoparticles by surface-enhanced Raman scattering. Science 275 (5303) (1997) 1102-1106.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly sable silver nanoplates for surface plasmon resonance biosensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Gao, Z. Lu, Y. Liu, Q. Zhang, M. Chi, Q. Cheng, et al., Highly sable silver nanoplates for surface plasmon resonance biosensing. Angew. Chem., Int. Ed. 51 (23) (2012) 5629-5633.
S. K. Bhunia, L. Zeiri, J. Manna, S. Nandi, R. Jelinek, Carbon-dot/silver-nanoparticle flexible SERS-active films. ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 8 (38) (2016) 25637-25643.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Remarkable SERS activity observed from amorphous ZnO nanocages

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Wang, W. Shi, Z. Jin, W. Huang, J. Lin, G. Ma, et al., Remarkable SERS activity observed from amorphous ZnO nanocages. Angew. Chem., Int. Ed. 56 (33) (2017) 9851-9855.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrasensitive SERS detection by defect engineering on single Cu2O superstructure particle

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Lin, Y. Shang, X. Li, J. Yu, X. Wang, L. Guo, Ultrasensitive SERS detection by defect engineering on single Cu2O superstructure particle. Adv. Mater. 29 (5) (2017) 1604797.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Composition-adjustable Ag-Au substitutional alloy microcages enabling tunable plasmon resonance for ultrasensitive SERS

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Freezing the self-assembly process of gold nanocrystals
